quizQuestion: According to the Arrhenius definition, an acid is a substance that ___ when dissolved in water.
explanation: Svante Arrhenius defined an acid as a substance that produces H+ (hydrogen) ions when dissolved in water, and a base as one that produces OH- (hydroxide) ions. This was a major step forward in the 1880s, though it is limited to aqueous solutions and cannot explain non-aqueous acid-base behavior.
statement: An Arrhenius acid produces H+ ions when dissolved in water.
wowFactor: Arrhenius proposed his ionic dissociation theory for his PhD thesis in 1884 — his committee initially gave him the lowest passing grade. He later won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the same work.
visualDescription: A flask of water with HCl dissolving into separated H+ and Cl- ions shown as colored spheres drifting apart, labeled with the Arrhenius definition
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: In the Brønsted-Lowry model, a base is defined as a ___.
explanation: Brønsted and Lowry (independently in 1923) defined a base as any species that accepts a proton (H+). This is broader than the Arrhenius model: NH3 is a Brønsted-Lowry base even though it contains no OH-. The paired acid donates the proton the base accepts.
statement: A Brønsted-Lowry base is a proton (H+) acceptor.
wowFactor: Johannes Brønsted and Thomas Lowry published nearly identical theories in the same year without knowing about each other — a remarkable case of simultaneous scientific discovery.
visualDescription: Diagram showing NH3 molecule accepting an H+ from water to form NH4+ and OH-, with arrows indicating proton transfer
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: related by transfer of one H+
quizQuestion: A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species that ___.
explanation: In a Brønsted-Lowry reaction, when an acid donates a proton, the species remaining is its conjugate base. They differ by exactly one H+. For example, CH3COOH and CH3COO- are a conjugate acid-base pair — the acid loses H+ to form the conjugate base.
statement: A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species that differ by exactly one proton (H+).
wowFactor: Every Brønsted-Lowry acid-base reaction involves two conjugate pairs simultaneously — the textbook reaction of acetic acid with water has four species forming two conjugate pairs at once.
visualDescription: A reaction equation showing CH3COOH losing H+ to become CH3COO-, with a bracket labeling the pair as 'conjugate acid-base pair'
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, HClO4
acceptableAlternatives: hydrochloric, hydrobromic, hydroiodic, nitric, sulfuric, perchloric acids
distractors: HCl, HF, HBr, HNO3, H2SO4, H3PO4
HCl, HBr, HI, H2CO3, H2SO4, HClO4
HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H3PO4, HClO4
HCl, HBr, HI, HNO2, H2SO4, HClO4
HF, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, HClO4
HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO3, HClO4
HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, HClO3
HCl, HBr, HIO3, HNO3, H2SO4, HClO4
quizQuestion: Which six acids are classified as strong acids that completely dissociate in aqueous solution?
explanation: The six strong acids are HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, and HClO4. Strong means they are essentially 100% dissociated in dilute aqueous solution. HF is a common trap — it is a WEAK acid despite being a hydrogen halide. H2SO4 is strong only for its first dissociation.
statement: The six strong acids — HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, and HClO4 — completely dissociate in aqueous solution.
wowFactor: Perchloric acid (HClO4) is so strong it can dissolve through many materials and is stored in special fume hoods. It caused a famous laboratory fire in 1947 in Los Angeles that destroyed an entire building.
visualDescription: Six chemical formula labels arranged in a grid, each showing complete ionization arrows separating into ions, with HF crossed out and labeled 'weak' beside them
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: alkali metal hydroxides and the heavier alkaline earth hydroxides
NaOH, KOH, LiOH, RbOH, CsOH, Ca(OH)2, Sr(OH)2, Ba(OH)2
quizQuestion: Which hydroxide compounds are classified as strong bases that fully dissociate in aqueous solution?
explanation: Strong bases fully dissociate into metal cations and OH- ions. Group 1 (alkali metal) hydroxides — LiOH, NaOH, KOH, RbOH, CsOH — are strong bases. Among Group 2, only Ca(OH)2, Sr(OH)2, and Ba(OH)2 are classified as strong; Mg(OH)2 is sparingly soluble and only weakly dissociates.
statement: Strong bases include all Group 1 hydroxides plus Ca(OH)2, Sr(OH)2, and Ba(OH)2.
wowFactor: Lye (NaOH) is so caustic it can dissolve organic tissue — it was used historically for soap-making and is still used today for drain cleaners. It reacts so vigorously with aluminum that it produces flammable hydrogen gas.
visualDescription: Periodic table excerpt highlighting Group 1 metals and Ca/Sr/Ba in Group 2, each connected by arrows to their hydroxide formulas showing complete dissociation
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: negative log of hydrogen ion concentration
quizQuestion: The mathematical definition of pH is ___.
explanation: pH = -log[H+] where [H+] is the molar concentration of hydrogen ions (equivalently hydronium ions, H3O+). The negative sign means that as [H+] increases (more acidic), pH decreases. A tenfold change in [H+] changes pH by exactly 1 unit.
statement: pH is defined as the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration: pH = -log[H+].
wowFactor: The 'p' in pH stands for the German word 'Potenz' meaning 'power' — a reference to the power-of-10 scale. The notation was invented by Danish chemist Søren Sørensen in 1909 to make enzyme research more practical.
visualDescription: A pH scale from 0 to 14 with color gradient (red to purple), showing the formula pH = -log[H+] at the top with example values marked
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: At 25°C, the ion-product constant of water, Kw = [H+][OH-], equals ___.
explanation: Kw = [H+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C. In pure water, [H+] = [OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-7 mol/L, giving a neutral pH of 7. Kw is temperature-dependent — at body temperature (37°C), Kw ≈ 2.4 × 10^-14, so neutral pH is slightly below 7.
statement: At 25°C, the water ion-product constant Kw = [H+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14.
wowFactor: Because Kw increases with temperature, 'neutral' pH at 37°C (body temp) is about 6.8, not 7.0 — meaning blood at pH 7.4 is actually more basic relative to the true neutral point at body temperature than at 25°C.
visualDescription: Two water molecules shown auto-ionizing into H3O+ and OH-, with the equilibrium expression Kw = [H+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14 labeled below
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: At 25°C, the sum of pH and pOH for any aqueous solution equals ___.
explanation: Because Kw = [H+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14, taking -log of both sides gives: -log[H+] + (-log[OH-]) = 14, so pH + pOH = 14. This relationship allows conversion between pH and pOH for any aqueous solution at 25°C.
statement: At 25°C, pH + pOH = 14 for any aqueous solution, derived from Kw = 1.0 × 10^-14.
wowFactor: This elegant relationship means you only ever need to find one value — pH or pOH — and you instantly know the other. It's the aqueous chemistry equivalent of knowing one piece of a puzzle automatically determines another.
visualDescription: A simple equation pH + pOH = 14 displayed with a two-sided scale/balance, one side showing acidic solutions and the other basic, balanced at the midpoint of 7
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: pH equals negative log of the acid concentration
use the initial concentration directly as [H+]
quizQuestion: To find the pH of a strong acid solution with initial concentration [HA], you use ___.
explanation: Because strong acids dissociate 100%, [H+] = initial acid concentration. For example, 0.050 M HCl gives [H+] = 0.050 M, so pH = -log(0.050) = 1.30. No equilibrium calculation is needed — the complete dissociation assumption holds for concentrations above ~10^-7 M.
statement: For a strong acid, pH = -log[HA] because complete dissociation means [H+] equals the initial acid concentration.
wowFactor: At extremely low concentrations (below ~10^-6 M), the H+ contributed by water autoionization becomes significant and you can no longer ignore it — the pH of a 10^-8 M HCl solution is NOT 8 but rather ~6.98.
visualDescription: A beaker labeled '0.050 M HCl' with arrows showing complete separation into H+ and Cl- ions, with pH = -log(0.050) = 1.30 calculated in the corner
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: find pOH first, then pH = 14 - pOH
acceptableAlternatives: calculate pOH from [OH-], then subtract from 14
distractors: use Ka of the base directly
find Kb first, then ICE table
quizQuestion: To find the pH of a strong base solution, the correct two-step method is to ___.
explanation: Strong bases fully dissociate, so [OH-] equals the base concentration (times the number of OH- per formula unit). Then pOH = -log[OH-], and finally pH = 14 - pOH. For example, 0.020 M NaOH → [OH-] = 0.020 M → pOH = 1.70 → pH = 12.30.
statement: For a strong base, calculate pOH = -log[OH-] first, then use pH = 14 - pOH to find the pH.
wowFactor: Ba(OH)2 releases two OH- per formula unit — a student trap. A 0.010 M Ba(OH)2 solution gives [OH-] = 0.020 M, not 0.010 M, so the pH is 12.30, not 12.00.
visualDescription: Step diagram: '0.020 M NaOH → [OH-] = 0.020 M → pOH = 1.70 → pH = 12.30', shown as a downward staircase of calculations
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: products over reactants for acid dissociation
quizQuestion: For the weak acid equilibrium HA ⇌ H+ + A-, the acid dissociation constant expression is ___.
explanation: Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA] is the equilibrium expression for weak acid dissociation. Pure liquids and solids are not included. A larger Ka value means the acid is stronger (more dissociated at equilibrium). The concentration of water is omitted because it is essentially constant in dilute solutions.
statement: The acid dissociation constant Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA] describes the equilibrium position of a weak acid in water.
wowFactor: Ka values span over 40 orders of magnitude — from near-zero for extremely weak acids to over 10^9 for the strongest known superacids, making the Ka scale one of the widest measurement ranges in all of chemistry.
visualDescription: An equilibrium equation HA ⇌ H+ + A- with the Ka fraction written below it: numerator [H+][A-] and denominator [HA], color-coded by species
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: product of Ka and Kb equals Kw
quizQuestion: For a conjugate acid-base pair, the relationship between their dissociation constants and Kw is ___.
explanation: For any conjugate acid-base pair (e.g., CH3COOH and CH3COO-), Ka × Kb = Kw = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C. This means if you know Ka for the acid, you can calculate Kb for the conjugate base, and vice versa. Stronger acids have weaker conjugate bases.
statement: For a conjugate acid-base pair, Ka × Kb = Kw = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C.
wowFactor: This relationship explains why strong acids have essentially zero base strength for their conjugate bases — HCl has Ka ≈ 10^7, so Cl- has Kb = 10^-14 / 10^7 = 10^-21, meaning chloride is effectively inert as a base.
visualDescription: A see-saw diagram with Ka on one side and Kb on the other, balanced by Kw in the middle, showing that as Ka increases, Kb decreases proportionally
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: The acid dissociation constant (Ka) for acetic acid (CH3COOH) at 25°C is ___.
explanation: Acetic acid (CH3COOH) has Ka = 1.8 × 10^-5 at 25°C. This small Ka confirms it is a weak acid — less than 2% dissociated in a typical 0.1 M solution. Its pKa = -log(1.8 × 10^-5) ≈ 4.74, which is the pH at the half-equivalence point of its titration.
statement: Acetic acid has Ka = 1.8 × 10^-5 at 25°C, indicating it is a weak acid with only partial dissociation.
wowFactor: Acetic acid is the compound that makes vinegar sour. Vinegar is typically 5% acetic acid by mass — yet because Ka is so small, even that small percentage barely ionizes, making vinegar far less corrosive than its acid label might suggest.
visualDescription: A molecule of CH3COOH partially dissociating in water, with Ka = 1.8 × 10^-5 labeled, alongside a pH scale showing its typical solution pH near 3
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: equilibrium [H+] divided by initial acid concentration, times 100
quizQuestion: The percent ionization of a weak acid is calculated as ___.
explanation: Percent ionization = ([H+]eq / [HA]initial) × 100%. It tells what fraction of the original weak acid has dissociated. Percent ionization increases as the solution is diluted — a counterintuitive result known as Ostwald's Dilution Law. For 0.10 M acetic acid, percent ionization ≈ 1.3%.
statement: Percent ionization = ([H+]equilibrium / [HA]initial) × 100%, and it increases as a weak acid solution is diluted.
wowFactor: Diluting a weak acid actually increases the percent ionization — adding water shifts the equilibrium toward more dissociation. This is why dilute vinegar (5% acetic acid in water) ionizes more percent-wise than concentrated glacial acetic acid.
visualDescription: Two beakers side by side — one concentrated and one dilute — with percent ionization values shown above each, the dilute one having a higher percentage despite lower [H+]
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: To calculate the pH of a weak acid solution given Ka, chemists set up a ___ to track concentrations at each stage of the equilibrium.
explanation: An ICE table organizes the Initial concentrations, the Change (±x) that occurs as the system reaches equilibrium, and the Equilibrium concentrations. For HA ⇌ H+ + A-, if initial [HA] = C and Ka is small enough, [H+] ≈ x ≈ √(Ka × C). The 5% rule validates whether the approximation is acceptable.
statement: An ICE table (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) is used to calculate the pH of a weak acid by tracking concentration changes to equilibrium.
wowFactor: The '5% rule' for the ICE approximation — checking that x is less than 5% of the initial concentration — is a shortcut chemists devised to avoid solving quadratic equations. If violated, you must use the full quadratic formula.
visualDescription: A three-row ICE table filled in for HA ⇌ H+ + A-, with 'C', '-x', 'x' values labeled, and the final Ka expression written below the table
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: NaCl and H2O with neutral pH
quizQuestion: When a strong acid reacts with a stoichiometrically equal amount of a strong base, the products are ___.
explanation: Strong acid + strong base → neutral salt + water. For example, HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O. The resulting NaCl solution has pH = 7 because neither Na+ nor Cl- undergoes hydrolysis — they are the conjugate of a strong base and strong acid respectively, both too weak to affect pH.
statement: A strong acid reacting with an equal amount of strong base produces a neutral salt and water with pH = 7.
wowFactor: Mixing equal moles of concentrated HCl and NaOH releases enough heat to boil a small amount of solution — the neutralization reaction of strong acids and bases is highly exothermic, releasing about 57 kJ per mole of water formed.
visualDescription: Equation HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O shown with pH meter reading 7.00 in the product solution, all on a clean white background
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: weak acid and its conjugate base
acceptableAlternatives: weak base and its conjugate acid
distractors: strong acid and its conjugate base
strong acid and strong base in equal amounts
weak acid and strong base in excess
strong acid and weak base in equal amounts
quizQuestion: A buffer solution is composed of a ___, which allows it to resist changes in pH.
explanation: A buffer contains a weak acid (HA) and its conjugate base (A-) in significant concentrations. When H+ is added, the conjugate base (A-) consumes it: A- + H+ → HA. When OH- is added, the weak acid neutralizes it: HA + OH- → A- + H2O. Both reactions shift the equilibrium slightly but maintain near-constant pH.
statement: A buffer consists of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and conjugate acid), resisting pH changes by consuming added acid or base.
wowFactor: Human blood is buffered at pH 7.35–7.45 primarily by the carbonic acid/bicarbonate system (H2CO3/HCO3-). A drop outside this range to pH 7.0 or above 7.8 can be fatal — the buffer system is literally keeping you alive.
visualDescription: A beaker labeled 'buffer' containing CH3COOH and CH3COO- molecules, with arrows showing added H+ being absorbed by CH3COO- and added OH- being absorbed by CH3COOH
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: When a weak acid reacts completely with a strong base, the resulting salt solution is ___.
explanation: A weak acid + strong base reaction (e.g., CH3COOH + NaOH → CH3COONa + H2O) produces the conjugate base of the weak acid as the salt anion. CH3COO- is a Brønsted-Lowry base and reacts with water (hydrolyzes): CH3COO- + H2O ⇌ CH3COOH + OH-. This produces OH-, making the solution basic (pH > 7).
statement: Weak acid + strong base produces a basic salt solution (pH > 7) because the conjugate base of the weak acid undergoes hydrolysis.
wowFactor: Baking soda (NaHCO3) is the salt of carbonic acid (a weak acid) and NaOH (a strong base) — this is why a baking soda solution has pH ≈ 8.3, making it gently basic and effective for settling upset stomachs.
visualDescription: Reaction of CH3COOH + NaOH producing CH3COONa, with a secondary hydrolysis arrow showing CH3COO- reacting with water to produce OH-, pH meter reading above 7
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: When a strong acid reacts completely with a weak base, the resulting salt solution produces a(n) ___ solution.
explanation: Strong acid + weak base → acidic salt solution. For example, HCl + NH3 → NH4Cl. The NH4+ cation is the conjugate acid of the weak base NH3, and it hydrolyzes: NH4+ + H2O ⇌ NH3 + H3O+. This releases H3O+, making the solution acidic (pH < 7). The Cl- anion does not hydrolyze.
statement: Strong acid + weak base produces an acidic salt solution (pH < 7) because the conjugate acid of the weak base releases H3O+ upon hydrolysis.
wowFactor: Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) — sold as a food additive called 'sal ammoniac' and used in salty licorice candy popular in Scandinavia — is slightly acidic in solution precisely because of this hydrolysis reaction.
visualDescription: Reaction of HCl + NH3 forming NH4Cl, with secondary hydrolysis showing NH4+ reacting with water to produce H3O+, pH meter reading below 7
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: the conjugate base neutralizes added H+; the weak acid neutralizes added OH-
acceptableAlternatives: conjugate base consumes H+, weak acid consumes OH-
A- absorbs H+ and HA absorbs OH-
distractors: the weak acid neutralizes added H+; the conjugate base neutralizes added OH-
water absorbs all added ions equally
the salt prevents any change in ion concentration
Kw shifts to compensate for the added acid or base
the strong ion is always neutralized first
the buffer converts H+ to H2 gas
hydrogen bonding locks H+ and OH- in place
pKa shifts to match the new pH
quizQuestion: A buffer resists pH change because ___.
explanation: In a buffer containing HA and A-: when H+ is added, it reacts with the conjugate base (A- + H+ → HA), converting added H+ to weak acid with minimal pH change. When OH- is added, it reacts with the weak acid (HA + OH- → A- + H2O), consuming the base. Both processes prevent large pH swings.
statement: A buffer resists pH change because the conjugate base (A-) neutralizes added H+ and the weak acid (HA) neutralizes added OH-.
wowFactor: Buffer capacity is greatest when [HA] = [A-], which occurs when pH = pKa. At this point, the buffer can absorb equal amounts of acid and base — it is the sweet spot of buffering, which is why the half-equivalence point is so important in titrations.
visualDescription: Split diagram showing: left side — H+ being added to buffer and A- molecules absorbing them forming HA; right side — OH- being added and HA molecules absorbing them forming A- and H2O
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

distractors: pH equals the pKb of the base
quizQuestion: At the equivalence point of a strong acid–strong base titration, the pH is ___.
explanation: At the equivalence point of strong acid + strong base, all acid and base have been neutralized, producing only a neutral salt and water. Neither the cation nor the anion of the salt undergoes hydrolysis, so the solution is neutral with pH = 7 at 25°C.
statement: The equivalence point of a strong acid–strong base titration has pH = 7 because the resulting salt undergoes no hydrolysis.
wowFactor: This is a classic exam trick: students often confuse equivalence point with endpoint. The equivalence point is a property of the chemistry; the endpoint is when the indicator changes. For a strong-strong titration, any indicator that changes between pH 4 and pH 10 works because the curve is so steep there.
visualDescription: A titration curve for strong acid vs. strong base showing the steep S-curve reaching exactly pH 7 at the equivalence point, with 'pH = 7' labeled with an arrow
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: At the equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration, the pH is ___.
explanation: At the equivalence point of weak acid + strong base, all the weak acid has been converted to its conjugate base (e.g., all CH3COOH → CH3COO-). The conjugate base then hydrolyzes in water: CH3COO- + H2O ⇌ CH3COOH + OH-, producing OH- and making the pH > 7.
statement: The equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration has pH > 7 because the conjugate base of the weak acid undergoes basic hydrolysis.
wowFactor: This is why you cannot use phenolphthalein (which changes at pH 8.2-10) for every titration without thought — it happens to work well for weak acid + strong base titrations but would be a poor choice if the equivalence point were below pH 8.
visualDescription: Titration curve for weak acid vs. strong base, showing the equivalence point above pH 7 with a dashed line, and phenolphthalein color change range shaded in pink nearby
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: pH equals pKa of the weak acid
quizQuestion: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration, the relationship between pH and pKa is ___.
explanation: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the weak acid has been neutralized, so [HA] = [A-]. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) = pKa + log(1) = pKa. This makes the half-equivalence point a simple and precise way to experimentally determine pKa from a titration curve.
statement: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, pH = pKa because [HA] = [A-] and the log term in Henderson-Hasselbalch equals zero.
wowFactor: Biochemists exploit this principle to measure the pKa of amino acid side chains and enzyme active sites by titrating tiny protein samples and identifying the midpoint. It is one of the most practical tools for understanding protein chemistry.
visualDescription: A titration curve with the half-equivalence point marked at exactly half the equivalence volume, with 'pH = pKa' labeled with arrows showing equal concentrations of HA and A- in solution
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

distractors: pH depends only on the acid volume
quizQuestion: At the equivalence point of a weak base–strong acid titration, the pH is ___.
explanation: At the equivalence point of weak base + strong acid (e.g., NH3 + HCl → NH4Cl), all base is converted to its conjugate acid (NH4+). NH4+ hydrolyzes: NH4+ + H2O ⇌ NH3 + H3O+, releasing H3O+ and making the pH < 7. The solution is acidic at the equivalence point.
statement: The equivalence point of a weak base–strong acid titration has pH < 7 because the conjugate acid of the weak base undergoes acidic hydrolysis.
wowFactor: This is the symmetric counterpart of the weak acid–strong base result. The asymmetry around pH 7 means that the same neutralization 'point' in different titration types spans a pH range from roughly 4 to 10, demanding careful indicator selection for each.
visualDescription: Titration curve for weak base vs. strong acid showing the equivalence point below pH 7, with methyl orange or bromocresol green indicator color change range shaded nearby
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: the indicator's pKa (color-change range) should overlap the equivalence point pH
acceptableAlternatives: choose an indicator whose transition range includes the equivalence point pH
indicator pKa should equal the equivalence point pH
distractors: always use phenolphthalein regardless of titration type
choose the indicator with the lowest Ka
indicator color should match the analyte color
use an indicator with pKa equal to 7 always
choose the strongest available indicator acid
indicator must change color before the equivalence point
use litmus paper for all acid-base titrations
the indicator should have the same concentration as the analyte
quizQuestion: When selecting an indicator for a titration, the key principle is that ___.
explanation: An indicator is a weak acid (HIn) whose acid and base forms have different colors. It changes color when pH ≈ pKa(HIn) ± 1. For accurate results, this color-change range must include the equivalence point pH. For strong-strong titrations (pH 7), any indicator between pH 4–10 works due to the steep curve; for weak acid-strong base (pH > 7), choose phenolphthalein (range 8.2–10).
statement: A titration indicator should be chosen so that its color-change pH range overlaps the equivalence point pH of the specific titration.
wowFactor: There are over 200 known acid-base indicators — from the familiar phenolphthalein to the exotic Alizarin Yellow R. Each is a weak acid with a different pKa, giving chemists fine-grained control over which pH signals the endpoint of a titration.
visualDescription: A chart comparing three indicators (methyl orange, bromothymol blue, phenolphthalein) with their color-change ranges on a pH axis, and three titration types each matched to the appropriate indicator
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8
